texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: PERMEATION OF  DICHLOROMETHANE, NITROETHANE AND TETRACHLOROETHYLENE THROUGH POLY  (HEXAFLUOROPROPYLENE-CO-TETRAFLUOROETHYLENE)
texts.text: PERMEATION OF  DICHLOROMETHANE, NITROETHANE AND TETRACHLOROETHYLENE THROUGH POLY  (HEXAFLUOROPROPYLENE-CO-TETRAFLUOROETHYLENE)

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Summary
texts.text: Summary

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  permeabilities  and diffusivities  of  dichloromethane,  nitroethane  and tetrachlororthylene in poly( hexafluoropropylene-co-tetrafluoroethylene) , Teflon@  FEP,  have been determined  III  a continuous-flow  permeation  cell over the range 20- 100' C. Linear Arrhenius plots  of the perme abilities and diffusivities  were obtained,  with the permeabilities,  but not the diffusivities,  showing a change of slope at the glass transition  temperature, T,.  Linear correlations  were found between the activation  energy for diffusion,  ED, and the van der Waals diameter of the diffusant, d. plotted either as d or d', and between ED and the logarithm of the pre-exponential  parameter for d&ixslon. IXlD,.
texts.text: The  permeabilities  and diffusivities  of  dichloromethane,  nitroethane  and tetrachlororthylene in poly( hexafluoropropylene-co-tetrafluoroethylene) , Teflon@  FEP,  have been determined  III  a continuous-flow  permeation  cell over the range 20- 100' C. Linear Arrhenius plots  of the perme abilities and diffusivities  were obtained,  with the permeabilities,  but not the diffusivities,  showing a change of slope at the glass transition  temperature, T,.  Linear correlations  were found between the activation  energy for diffusion,  ED, and the van der Waals diameter of the diffusant, d. plotted either as d or d', and between ED and the logarithm of the pre-exponential  parameter for d&ixslon. IXlD,.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solubility  of the permeants calculated from these measurements compares reasonably well with that determined  gravimetrically  below T, at 30°C. However,  application  of the dual  mode sorption theory to the gravimetric sorption  isotherms shows that sorption by the Langmuu  mode at  this  temperature  only  makes  a small  contribution  to  the  greater solubility  exhibited  hb the polymer in the glassy state.
texts.text: The solubility  of the permeants calculated from these measurements compares reasonably well with that determined  gravimetrically  below T, at 30°C. However,  application  of the dual  mode sorption theory to the gravimetric sorption  isotherms shows that sorption by the Langmuu  mode at  this  temperature  only  makes  a small  contribution  to  the  greater solubility  exhibited  hb the polymer in the glassy state.

texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Glassy polymers  have a potential  in thin  film composite  membranes  for  gas and vapor  separation,  and a greater understanding  is needed  of their permea bility  and  selectivity [ 11. Membranes  of  poly (tetrafluoroethylene-ctr hexafluoropropylene) have been found to be useful for the constructl!m of permeation  tubes,  providing inexpensive and  reliable  calibrated  sources  for gases and vapors [ 2 1, and also as a selective  interface  between  a stack  gas and a continuous  analyzer [ 31. The  transport  of  various  organic  gases and \  apors through  membranes  of this copolymer  has been the subject of previous  studies, which  have  included  the  lower  alkanes,  benzene  and  methyl  chloride !  ,1  S] The  present  study  concerns  the permeation  of  dichloromethane, nit  roe1  hane

texts.orig: The  efficiency of  the  saturator  was  determined  for  each  permrant, at  the particular  saturator  temperature  and  carrier  gas flow  rate emploved~ hv  coletting  the  vapor  in  a cold  trap  over  a known  period  of  time.  The  flame  roni~ zation  detector  was calibrated  for  dichloromethane, nitroethane  and methane by the use of  a second  gas-chromatographic sampling  valve  (V i fitted  with a sample loop,  enabling  a known  amount  of  the  saturated  vapor  or methane to be passed to the detector.  With  tetrachloroethylene the peaks prodrir>ed on the coupled  recorder  had  inconveniently long  tails  and  calibration  was achieved by passing  a mixture  of  12.5 ppm tetrachloroethylene in nitrogen to the detec-tor to produce  a constant  signal height.
texts.text: The  efficiency of  the  saturator  was  determined  for  each  permrant, at  the particular  saturator  temperature  and  carrier  gas flow  rate emploved~ hv  coletting  the  vapor  in  a cold  trap  over  a known  period  of  time.  The  flame  roni~ zation  detector  was calibrated  for  dichloromethane, nitroethane  and methane by the use of  a second  gas-chromatographic sampling  valve  (V i fitted  with a sample loop,  enabling  a known  amount  of  the  saturated  vapor  or methane to be passed to the detector.  With  tetrachloroethylene the peaks prodrir>ed on the coupled  recorder  had  inconveniently long  tails  and  calibration  was achieved by passing  a mixture  of  12.5 ppm tetrachloroethylene in nitrogen to the detec-tor to produce  a constant  signal height.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  equilibrium  absorption of  the  permeants  by  the  copolymer film  was determined  using  a Sartorius  Model 4102 electronic  vacuum  microhalance  in conjunction  with a Texas  Instruments  quartz Bourdon  pressure gauge allowing monitoring of  weight  and  pressure  changes  to  5 0.01  mg  and + 0 lb1 mmHg respectively.  The  apparatus has been described previously  [  91. Abscrrpt  ion iso- therms were determined  on samples of ca. 1.8 g of the copolymer  film, the 0.001 in. thick  film being  used in preference  to the 0.002 in. film used in t  he dynamic measurements  to reduce the time to reach equilibrium.  A Sartorius Model  4201 magnetic  suspension  balance  coupled  with  a MKS  instruments  t\pe 270/370 Baratron  pressure  gauge  was  used  to  determine  the  diffusion coefficient of dichloromethane in the 0.001 in. thick  copolymer  film by following  the rate of sorption.  This  allowed  weight  changes  to be  monitored  to  -+  0.1  mg on  a 20 g sample  of  the  0.001  in.  film  and  the  pressure  to  be  measured  to + 0.1  mmHg. This  apparatus  has also been  described  previously [lo].

This  allowed  weight  changes  to be  monitored  to  -+  0.1  mg on  a 20 g sample  of  the  0.001  in.  film  and  the  pressure  to  be  measured  to + 0.1  mmHg. This  apparatus  has also been  described  previously [lo].

[ 71, where 1 is  the membrane thickness  and d (AS)  /dt  is the  slope of the linear part of the experimental curve  displayed  on  the  recorder  as steady-state  permeation  is approached. The permeation  of the copolymer  to methane  was determined  init.ially in order to check  the  apparatus,  as this  particular  system  had been  studied  by several previous investigators [ 3-51.  Measurements were made with a mixture of 2.05% methane  in nitrogen  over the temperature  ranges 100 to 60'  C and 50 to 20' C, at approximately 10' C intervals.  The  values  of  the  activation  energy  for  permeation, E,, and  the  pre-exponential factor, PO, for  the  Arrhenius relation expressing the variation of the permeability constant P with temperature, P  = PO exp (  -EJRT) , and  the  permeability at 90' C are  given  in  Table I,  together with the results of the previous  investigators. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  permeability parameters determined for  dichloromethane, nitroethane and  tetrachloroethylene are  reported  in  Table  2. Variation of the  partial  pressure  of  the  permeants in  the  pressure range  studied  had  no  discernible effect on the  transport properties determined, the  flux  of the  permeant being  directly proportional to  the  permeant partial pressure. The Arrhenius plots of  the permeabilities in  the  higher  temperature range  are  shown  in  Fig.  2. There is a distinct change  of  slope  in these  plots  at  T=  346.6  K  in the  region  of the  glass transition temperature, T,, which  decreases with  increasing hexafluoropropylene  content and  is  reported  to  be  about  75°C for  14  mol%  hexafluoropropylene [ 111. The discontinuity is  most marked for  the largest permeant, tetrachloroethylene, and is least  apparent  with the  smallest, dichloromet,hane. The  van  der  Waals  volumes  for  methane, dichloromethane, nitroethane and tetrachloroethylene are  28.4, 57.6,  67.6  and  94  A3, respectively [  121. Such  a discontinuity  was not detected  with methane  over the 60-100' C range, although a higher  value  of Ep was determined  than  that  for the  lower temperature range (see  Table 1).
texts.text: The  permeability parameters determined for  dichloromethane, nitroethane and  tetrachloroethylene are  reported  in  Table  2. Variation of the  partial  pressure  of  the  permeants in  the  pressure range  studied  had  no  discernible effect on the  transport properties determined, the  flux  of the  permeant being  directly proportional to  the  permeant partial pressure. The Arrhenius plots of  the permeabilities in  the  higher  temperature range  are  shown  in  Fig.  2. There is a distinct change  of  slope  in these  plots  at  T=  346.6  K  in the  region  of the  glass transition temperature, T,, which  decreases with  increasing hexafluoropropylene  content and  is  reported  to  be  about  75°C for  14  mol%  hexafluoropropylene [ 111. The discontinuity is  most marked for  the largest permeant, tetrachloroethylene, and is least  apparent  with the  smallest, dichloromet,hane. The  van  der  Waals  volumes  for  methane, dichloromethane, nitroethane and tetrachloroethylene are  28.4, 57.6,  67.6  and  94  A3, respectively [  121. Such  a discontinuity  was not detected  with methane  over the 60-100' C range, although a higher  value  of Ep was determined  than  that  for the  lower temperature range (see  Table 1).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  results  of the  diffusion  measurements are  reported  in Table  3 and those for  the  60-100°C range  are  shown  in  Fig.  3  in  the  form  of  the  Arrhenius plot relating  the  diffusion  coefficient, D, to  a pre-exponential constant,,  D,,  and the activation energy for diffusion, ED, according to the relation D =DO exp (  - EJRT) .  Surprisingly, no  discontinuities are  apparent on  these  plots, and  little  difference was  found  between  the ED values  determined for  a  diffusant  in the  higher  and lower temperature ranges.  Pasternak et al. [ 41  measured permeation and  diffusion coefficients for  several  hydrocarbons in  the  copolymer  over  the  range  25  to  95°C. Although  insufficient low temperature measurements were  made to locate T,, permeation and  diffusion coefficients

texts.label: text
texts.orig: determined  at 95'  C were seen to be significantly  above  a linear Arhenius  plot, and the authors  suspected  the presence  of  a phase  transition.  Duncan,  Koros and Felder [ 61  obtained  linear  Arrhenius  plots  for  permeation and diffusion for  methyl  chloride  and benzene  in the  copolymer  over  the range 47 to  100°C with no  change  in gradient  at T,  for  either  permeant.  The  van  der Waals  volumes  for  these  molecules  are  42.0  and  80.3  A3, respectively [ 121.  That  for methyl chloride  lies between the values for methane and dichloromethane,  and, considering  the  small change  in the  gradient  shown  in Fig.  2 for  dichloromethane,  it is not surprising that a discontinuity  was not  detected.  However,  benzene has a greater  molecular  volume  than  nitroethane,  and  a change  of  slope might have been  expected.  As far as the authors  are aware, in previous  studies where a change  of  slope  in the Arrhenius  plot  for permeation  has been  found, it  has  been  accompanied by  change  of  slope  in  a  similar  plot  for  diffusion. Indeed the discontinuity  in permeation  at the glass transition  temperature  has often  been  attributed  to  the  relative  size  of  holes  or  free  volume  elements  in

Indeed the discontinuity  in permeation  at the glass transition  temperature  has often  been  attributed  to  the  relative  size  of  holes  or  free  volume  elements  in

texts.orig: As  a check  on  the  diffusion coefficients determined,  that  for  dichloromethane  was measured by  following  the rate of  sorption  on a microbalance.  Five determinations were  made  at  approximately 5  cmHg  intervals  from  0 to  25 cmHg  at  29.81'C, and  are  reported  in  Table  4,  together  with  the  weight  of penetrant  sorbed at equilibrium, M,. Each diffusion  coefficient  was calculated from the initial gradient of M,, the weight of penetrant  sorbed  at time t, plotted against  t  l' according to  the  theoretical equation  for  diffusion into  a  plane sheet at short times after an instantaneous  change  in pressure [ 141'  A typical plot  for  one  of  the  determinations is  shown  in  Fig.  4. texts.orig: with the value of 3.22 x lo-' cm*-set-l calculated from the parameters in Table 3 representing  the diffusion  measurements  from the dynamic  method.  According to Yasuda  and Hirotsu [ 131 a change  of  slope  in the Arrhenius  plot  of the permeability  at Tg will be seen only  if the diffusion  coefficient at Tg is smaller than  a certain  value (5 x lOPa cm*-sec-l) . Interestingly,  the  present  results appear  to  be  in  approximate agreement  with  this  finding.  Taking T, for  the copolymer  as 73.5'  C, the values of D at Tg are  (0.83,2.9, and 5.1) x lo-* cm*set-' for tetrachloroethylene, nitroethane  and dichloromethane, respectively, while  a value  of  D  for  methane,  which  does  not  show  a  change  of  slope,  of 2.6~  10e7  cm*-see-1 may be calculated  from  the  results of  Yi-Yan  et al. [ 31. The  diffusion  coefficient for  benzene  at 73.5'  C calculated  from  the  results of Duncan  et al. [ 61 is 1.2 x lOPa cm*-set-I, which  again suggests that  a change of  slope  might  have been  expected  in the permeation  plot.

texts.orig: Duncan  et al. [ 61 reported  a linear correlation  between  the activation  energy for  diffusion,  En,  and  the  square of  the  Lennard-Jones collision  diameter  for the penetrants  they studied, with only  propane  and benzene  deviating  from  it. Figure  5  shows  a  similar  correlation  between ED and  d*  for  the  penetrants previously  studied [ 3,  61  and  those  studied  here.  The  molecular  diameter  d used in this correlation  is that calculated  from  the van  der Waals volume [ 121 assuming the volume  to be spherical,  since Lennard-Jones diameters were not available  for  nitroethane  and  tetrachloroethylene. It  can  be  seen  from  Fig.  5 that none  of the diffusants  shows a significant  deviation  from this correlation.

Figure  5  shows  a  similar  correlation  between ED and  d*  for  the  penetrants previously  studied [ 3,  61  and  those  studied  here.  The  molecular  diameter  d used in this correlation  is that calculated  from  the van  der Waals volume [ 121 assuming the volume  to be spherical,  since Lennard-Jones diameters were not available  for  nitroethane  and  tetrachloroethylene. texts.orig: Combining  the measurements  for the permeability  coefficient  and diffusion coefficient  allows the solubility  coefficient, S, to be evaluated,  as P = SD if the usual  assumptions  are made  that  the  solubility  can  be  expressed  by  Henry's Law and that Fick's  Laws of  diffusion  are obeyed.  The  solubility  can then  be expressed  in  the  form,  S=  S,  exp (  -dHs/RT) , where Ep  =&I, + ED. The calculated  parameter  S,, and  the  heat  of  solution  MS for  each  permeant  are
texts.text: Combining  the measurements  for the permeability  coefficient  and diffusion coefficient  allows the solubility  coefficient, S, to be evaluated,  as P = SD if the usual  assumptions  are made  that  the  solubility  can  be  expressed  by  Henry's Law and that Fick's  Laws of  diffusion  are obeyed.  The  solubility  can then  be expressed  in  the  form,  S=  S,  exp (  -dHs/RT) , where Ep  =&I, + ED. The calculated  parameter  S,, and  the  heat  of  solution  MS for  each  permeant  are

texts.label: text
texts.orig: presented in  Table 5.  Because the  Arrhenius plots for  diffusion were  linear throughout the  temperature range  studied, the  change of  slope  at  7',  in  the Arrhenius  plots  for  permeation is reflected in the  solubilities calculated. Thus the heat of  solution is  more  exothermic for  each  permeant below  the  glass transition temperature. As the  solubility of  a vapor  in  a polymer is  related  to its  condensability, the  logarithm of  the  solubility frequently has  a  linear correlation with  the  normal  boiling  point.  Yi-Yan et  al.  found  such  a correlation for the  straight-chain hydrocarbons they  studied  in  Teflon  FEP [ 31.  Figure  7 shows that  the  solubilities of  nitroethane and  dichloromethane, calculated for the  purposes  of  comparison at  9O'C,  agree  reasonably well  with  t.his correlation, but  that for  tetrachloroethylene shows  an  even  greater deviation than that  shown  by  methylchloride or  benzene studied  by  Duncan et  al. [ 61.  The heat  of  solution, AH,, above  the  glass  transition temperature is  the  sum  of  a heat  of  condensation and  a heat  of  mixing.  Although  tetrachloroethylene and nitroethane have  similar  boiling  points and  enthalpies of  vaporisation [ 161, the  polarity of  nitroethane will produce  a greater  endothermic heat  of mixing and  therefore a  lower  exothermic heat  of  solution, causing  a  lower  solubility than  shown  by tetrachloroethylene.
texts.text: presented in  Table 5. Thus the heat of  solution is  more  exothermic for  each  permeant below  the  glass transition temperature. As the  solubility of  a vapor  in  a polymer is  related  to its  condensability, the  logarithm of  the  solubility frequently has  a  linear correlation with  the  normal  boiling  point.  Yi-Yan et  al.  found  such  a correlation for the  straight-chain hydrocarbons they  studied  in  Teflon  FEP [ 31.  Figure  7 shows that  the  solubilities of  nitroethane and  dichloromethane, calculated for the  purposes  of  comparison at  9O'C,  agree  reasonably well  with  t.his correlation, but  that for  tetrachloroethylene shows  an  even  greater deviation than that  shown  by  methylchloride or  benzene studied  by  Duncan et  al. [ 61.  The heat  of  solution, AH,, above  the  glass  transition temperature is  the  sum  of  a heat  of  condensation and  a heat  of  mixing.  Although  tetrachloroethylene and nitroethane have  similar  boiling  points and  enthalpies of  vaporisation [ 161, the  polarity of  nitroethane will produce  a greater  endothermic heat  of mixing and  therefore a  lower  exothermic heat  of  solution, causing  a  lower  solubility than  shown  by tetrachloroethylene.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to examine  the validity  of the solubilities  calculated  from the  dynamic measurements, the  absorption in  the  copolymer film  of  dichloromethane was determined at  30°C using  a  vacuum  microbalance. The  isotherms shown  in Fig.  8  have  a  slight initial curvature, concave to  the  pressure axis,  which  is indicative of  dual-mode sorption. The curvature resulting from  dual-mode sorption increases as  the  isothermal temperature decreases below  that of  the glass  transition temperature [  171. In  the  dual-mode sorption  theory  the  total concentration absorbed, C, is  regarded  as  the  sum  of  a contribution from  the dissolved  species  obeying  Henry's Law,  Cn,  and  a  contribution from  sorption into  pre-existing microvoids  or holes  governed  by  the  Langmuir isotherm, CH:

The curvature resulting from  dual-mode sorption increases as  the  isothermal temperature decreases below  that of  the glass  transition temperature [  171. In  the  dual-mode sorption  theory  the  total concentration absorbed, C, is  regarded  as  the  sum  of  a contribution from  the dissolved  species  obeying  Henry's Law,  Cn,  and  a  contribution from  sorption into  pre-existing microvoids  or holes  governed  by  the  Langmuir isotherm, CH:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Assuming  the  application of  the  theory  to  these  isotherms, the  limiting slope at  higher  pressures gives  the  Henry's Law  constant, kn. For  dichloromethane the  values  of  kn determined for  the  film  'as  received',  annealed at  100 'C  and 200°C were  0.133, 0.129  and  0.109  mg  CH,Cl,-cmHgg'-g polymer  -l, respectively.  Taking the  weight  per  cent crystallinity in  the 'as  received film  and lOO'C-annealed film to  be  55%, as  determined from  the density measurements,  the  mean kD values  for these  films  gives a value of k,, = 0.294 mg CH&12cmHg-l-g-' of amorphous  polymer.  Application of this  to the  kb value  for  the 200'C-annealed film  shows  that  the  film  contains 37  wt.%  of amorphous  polymer,  supporting  the  value  of 62.2  wt.%  crystallinity obtained  from the  density measurements, and  confirming the  earlier  conclusions on the  increase in crystallinity on annealing at  200 o  C.  The  sorption  measurements, like  the  density measurements, show  that  there  is  little  increase in  crystallinity on  annealing at  1OO'C.
texts.text: Assuming  the  application of  the  theory  to  these  isotherms, the  limiting slope at  higher  pressures gives  the  Henry's Law  constant, kn. For  dichloromethane the  values  of  kn determined for  the  film  'as  received',  annealed at  100 'C  and 200°C were  0.133, 0.129  and  0.109  mg  CH,Cl,-cmHgg'-g polymer  -l, respectively.  Taking the  weight  per  cent crystallinity in  the 'as  received film  and lOO'C-annealed film to  be  55%, as  determined from  the density measurements,  the  mean kD values  for these  films  gives a value of k,, = 0.294 mg CH&12cmHg-l-g-' of amorphous  polymer.  Application of this  to the  kb value  for  the 200'C-annealed film  shows  that  the  film  contains 37  wt.%  of amorphous  polymer,  supporting  the  value  of 62.2  wt.%  crystallinity obtained  from the  density measurements, and  confirming the  earlier  conclusions on the  increase in crystallinity on annealing at  200 o  C.  The  sorption  measurements, like  the  density measurements, show  that  there  is  little  increase in  crystallinity on  annealing at  1OO'C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A comparison is shown  in Table  6 of the  solubilities at  30'  C calculated  from the  dynamic  measurements obtained below  the  glass  transition temperature, reported  in Table  5, with  those  determined from the  gravimetric sorption  measurements on the  100'  C-annealed film.  The  partial  pressures used  in the  permeation/diffusion determinations corresponded to  relative  pressures at  30'  C of  0.49,  0.38  and  0.41  for  dichloromethane, nitroethane and  tetrachloroethylene,  respectively. The  gravimetric sorption isotherms were  recorded  to  a relative  pressure of  ca.  0.25,  and  the  gravimetric solubilities reported  in  Table 6 were  calculated on the  assumption that  the  sorption  isotherms (see  Fig.  8 for dichloromethane) could  be  extrapolated linearly to  the  higher  relative pressure.  Subsequent sorption measurements reported in  Table 4  verify  this for dichloromethane. If  the  P/D  measurements above Tg are  extrapolated below T, to estimate solubilities at  3O'C, much  smaller  values  are obtained,  and these are  reported in  the  final  column  in  Table 6.  The solubilities calculated from the P/D measurements below Tg  for dichloromethane, nitroethane and
texts.text: A comparison is shown  in Table  6 of the  solubilities at  30'  C calculated  from the  dynamic  measurements obtained below  the  glass  transition temperature, reported  in Table  5, with  those  determined from the  gravimetric sorption  measurements on the  100'  C-annealed film.  The  partial  pressures used  in the  permeation/diffusion determinations corresponded to  relative  pressures at  30'  C of  0.49,  0.38  and  0.41  for  dichloromethane, nitroethane and  tetrachloroethylene,  respectively. The  gravimetric sorption isotherms were  recorded  to  a relative  pressure of  ca.  0.25,  and  the  gravimetric solubilities reported  in  Table 6 were  calculated on the  assumption that  the  sorption  isotherms (see  Fig.  8 for dichloromethane) could  be  extrapolated linearly to  the  higher  relative pressure.  Subsequent sorption measurements reported in  Table 4  verify  this for dichloromethane. If  the  P/D  measurements above Tg are  extrapolated below T, to estimate solubilities at  3O'C, much  smaller  values  are obtained,  and these are  reported in  the  final  column  in  Table 6.  The solubilities calculated from the P/D measurements below Tg  for dichloromethane, nitroethane and

texts.label: text
texts.orig: tetrachloroethylene are  43, 70  and  89% greater than these, reflecting the increased  solubility  from the  polymer  in the  glassy  state.  However,  the  sorption isotherms determined gravimetrically showed  only  a slight  concave  curvature to  the  pressure axis, as  can  be  seen in  Fig.  8  for  dichloromethane. Hence, adsorption  into  holes  and cavities  in the  glassy polymer  by the  Langmuir  mode, calculated by  applying  eqn. (6) to  the  isotherms, makes  a very  small  eontribution  to  the  greater solubilities found  below Tg at  30°C.
texts.text: tetrachloroethylene are  43, 70  and  89% greater than these, reflecting the increased  solubility  from the  polymer  in the  glassy  state.  However,  the  sorption isotherms determined gravimetrically showed  only  a slight  concave  curvature to  the  pressure axis, as  can  be  seen in  Fig.  8  for  dichloromethane. Hence, adsorption  into  holes  and cavities  in the  glassy polymer  by the  Langmuir  mode, calculated by  applying  eqn. (6) to  the  isotherms, makes  a very  small  eontribution  to  the  greater solubilities found  below Tg at  30°C.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Conclusions
texts.text: Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  permeabilities, unlike the  diffusivities, of  the  three  permeants in  the copolymer  increase with  molecular size.  This results from  the  differing solubilities of  the  permeants, with  the  most  condensable vapor,  tetrachloroethylene (b.pt. 121aC), having a  greater solubility than the less  condensable, dichloromethane (b.pt. 40 o  C )  . Although  nitroethane (b.pt.  115 O C ) has  a similar  condensability to  tetrachloroethylene, as  measured  by  boiling  point  and enthalpy of  condensation, it  has  a lower  solubility and  hence  a lower  permeability.  This decrease in  solubility can  be  explained by  the  polarity of  nitroethane  giving  rise  to  a greater  endothermic heat  of mixing  than  would be  given by tetrachloroethylene.
texts.text: The  permeabilities, unlike the  diffusivities, of  the  three  permeants in  the copolymer  increase with  molecular size.  This results from  the  differing solubilities of  the  permeants, with  the  most  condensable vapor,  tetrachloroethylene (b.pt. 121aC), having a  greater solubility than the less  condensable, dichloromethane (b.pt. 40 o  C )  . Although  nitroethane (b.pt.  115 O C ) has  a similar  condensability to  tetrachloroethylene, as  measured  by  boiling  point  and enthalpy of  condensation, it  has  a lower  solubility and  hence  a lower  permeability.  This decrease in  solubility can  be  explained by  the  polarity of  nitroethane  giving  rise  to  a greater  endothermic heat  of mixing  than  would be  given by tetrachloroethylene.

texts.orig: The  authors are  grateful  to  Professor F.S. Stone for  his  continued interest and support  throughout this  research, and wish to thank  Mr  R.I.M. Coutts  for his  assistance with  the  measurement of  diffusion coefficients by  the  sorption method.
texts.text: The  authors are  grateful  to  Professor F.S. Stone for  his  continued interest and support  throughout this  research, and wish to thank  Mr  R.I.M. Coutts  for his  assistance with  the  measurement of  diffusion coefficients by  the  sorption method.
